questions.text: Which of the following factors is NOT required to obtain the maximum height of a projectile?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The mass of the projectile does not directly affect the maximum height it can reach. The vertical motion of a projectile is determined by its initial velocity and the gravitational force acting on it. The mass of the projectile affects its trajectory and the range it can achieve, but it does not impact the maximum height.

The launch angle of the projectile is crucial in determining the maximum height. Launching the projectile at an angle close to 90 degrees (vertical launch) maximizes the height reached. As the launch angle deviates from 90 degrees, the vertical component of the initial velocity decreases, resulting in a lower maximum height.

The initial velocity of the projectile significantly influences the maximum height. A higher initial velocity will generally result in a greater maximum height. This is because a higher velocity provides more upward motion against the force of gravity, allowing the projectile to reach higher altitudes.

The gravitational acceleration affects the vertical motion of the projectile. It pulls the projectile downwards and determines the rate at which the projectile decelerates vertically. However, the value of gravitational acceleration (approximately 9.8 m/s² on Earth) is constant and does not impact the maximum height directly.

questions.explanation: The mass of the projectile is irrelevant to the maximum height it can reach, as maximum height is dictated by the initial vertical velocity and gravitational pull. Mass affects the overall trajectory and range, not the apex height. In contrast, the angle and initial velocity are critical to achieving maximum height, as they determine the initial vertical velocity. Gravitational acceleration, while constant, affects the rate of ascent and descent, influencing the height indirectly by acting against the initial velocity.

questions.text: An object is thrown with velocity v from the edge of a cliff above level ground. Neglect air resistance. In order for the object to travel a maximum horizontal distance from the cliff before hitting the ground, the throw should be at an angle 0 with respect to the horizontal of
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: An angle greater than 60° results in a higher vertical component of velocity, reducing the horizontal range significantly due to a large upward motion.

options.text: greater than 45° but less than 60° above the horizontal
options.explanation: An angle in this range provides a relatively high vertical component, which still limits the horizontal distance compared to lower angles.

options.text: greater than zero but less than 45° above the horizontal
options.explanation: The 45° angle gives the maximum horizontal travel to the original elevation, but the smaller angle causes the projectile to have a greater horizontal component of velocity, so given the additional time of travel allows such a trajectory to advance a greater horizontal distance. In other words given enough time the smaller angle of launch gives a parabola which will eventual cross the parabola of the 45° launch.

options.text: greater than zero but less than 45° below the horizontal
options.explanation: An angle below horizontal minimizes the vertical component, causing the object to hit the ground sooner, thus reducing the horizontal range.

questions.explanation: In projectile motion, the optimal angle for maximum horizontal distance when launching from an elevation is less than 45°. While 45° is optimal for maximum range on a flat plane, launching from a height means a smaller angle provides a longer time in the air and a larger horizontal velocity component, increasing the total horizontal distance. Angles greater than 45° increase the vertical component, reducing range, and angles below the horizontal severely limit both time in the air and horizontal distance. Therefore, an angle greater than zero but less than 45° above the horizontal is ideal.

questions.text: A rock is dropped from the top of a tall tower. Half a second later another rock, twice as massive as the first, is dropped. Ignoring air resistance.
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The distance between the rocks increases while both are falling.
options.explanation: The distance between the rocks increases while both are falling: This is true. While both rocks are accelerating downwards due to gravity at the same rate (approximately 9.8 m/s²), the first rock has a head start of half a second. This means it has already covered some distance before the second rock is even released. Since both rocks continue to accelerate at the same rate, the distance between them will continue to increase as they fall. The first rock will always be ahead because it was released first.

options.text: The acceleration is greater for the more massive rock.
options.explanation: False: The acceleration due to gravity is constant (9.8 m/s²) for all objects, regardless of mass, when air resistance is ignored.

options.text: They strike the ground more than half a second apart.
options.explanation: False: Since both rocks experience the same gravitational acceleration, the time gap between their impacts equals the time gap of their releases, which is half a second.

options.text: They strike the ground with the same kinetic energy.
options.explanation: False: Kinetic energy depends on both mass and velocity. Although both rocks reach the same velocity upon impact, the second rock, having twice the mass, will have twice the kinetic energy.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A: The distance between the rocks increases while both are falling. The first rock is released before the second and starts accelerating immediately due to gravity. When the second rock is released, it begins to fall with the same acceleration but starts from rest, whereas the first rock already has velocity. Consequently, the distance between them increases. Option B is incorrect because all objects accelerate at the same rate in free fall regardless of mass. Option C is incorrect because the time difference between the releases is maintained until impact. Option D is incorrect because the second rock, being more massive, will strike the ground with greater kinetic energy.

questions.text: A spring-loaded gun can fire a projectile to a height h if it is fired straight up. If the same gun is pointed at an angle of 45° from the vertical, what maximum height can now be reached by the projectile?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option assumes an incorrect calculation of the vertical component of the velocity at a 45° launch angle. The height is not reduced to a quarter; the vertical component needs to be considered.
This answer miscalculates the effect of the angle on the vertical component of velocity. The reduction factor is not correct.

Using energy conservation in the first situation presented K=U gives the initial velocity as v = √2gh . The gun will fire at this velocity regardless of the angle. In the second scenario, the ball starts with the same initial energy but at the top will have both KE and PE so will be at a lower height. The velocity at the top will be equal to the vx at the beginning.
This option misrepresents the effect of the angle on the vertical component. The factor indicates a misunderstanding of the correct trigonometric application.

questions.explanation: When the projectile is fired straight up, all of its initial velocity contributes to reaching the height h. At an angle of 45°, only the vertical component of the velocity (v_y = v * sin(45°)) contributes to the maximum height. Since sin(45°) = 1/√2, the vertical component becomes v/√2. Using the formula for maximum height, (v_y^2)/2g, and substituting v_y with v/√2, the height becomes (v^2/2g) * (1/2) = h/2. Thus, the correct answer is h/2. Other options incorrectly modify the vertical component or misapply the trigonometric relationships.

questions.text: An object is projected vertically upward from ground level. It rises to a maximum height H. If air resistance is negligible, which of the following must be true for the object when it is at a height H/2?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Its speed is half of its initial speed.
options.explanation: This is incorrect because speed does not linearly decrease by half at half the maximum height. The relationship between speed and height is not linear due to the conservation of energy.

options.text: Its kinetic energy is half of its initial kinetic energy.
options.explanation: Energy is conserved so the term mgh + ½ mv2 must remain constant. As the object rises it loses K and gains U. Since the height is H/2 it has gained half of the total potential energy it will end up with which means it must have lost half of its kinetic energy, so its K is half of what it was when it was first shot.

options.text: Its potential energy is half of its initial potential energy.
options.explanation: This is incorrect because the initial potential energy at ground level is zero. At height H/2, the potential energy is half of the maximum potential energy it would have at height H, not half of its initial potential energy.

options.text: Its total mechanical energy is half of its initial value.
options.explanation: This is incorrect because total mechanical energy is conserved in the absence of air resistance, so it remains constant throughout the motion.

questions.explanation: In the absence of air resistance, the total mechanical energy of the object is conserved, which means it remains constant throughout the object's motion. The total mechanical energy is the sum of kinetic energy (K) and potential energy (U). Initially, at ground level, the object has maximum kinetic energy and zero potential energy. As it rises, it loses kinetic energy and gains potential energy.When the object reaches a height of H/2, it has gained half of the maximum potential energy it will achieve at height H. Consequently, it must have lost half of its initial kinetic energy to maintain the conservation of total mechanical energy. Therefore, the correct statement is that its kinetic energy is half of its initial kinetic energy.Option A is incorrect because speed is not directly halved at half the maximum height. Option C is incorrect because the initial potential energy is zero, and potential energy at any height is measured from that initial state. Option D is incorrect because the total mechanical energy remains constant, not halved.

questions.text: A handball is tossed vertically upward with a velocity of 19.6 meter per second. Approximately how high will it rise?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The calculation leading to 15m likely involves an error in applying the kinematic equation for maximum height.
This is the correct answer. The kinematic equation correctly applied gives a maximum height of 20m.
Reaching 25m incorrectly uses the value for gravitational acceleration, leading to an overestimation.
Estimating 30m ignores the decelerating effect of gravity, resulting in a significant overestimation.

questions.explanation: To determine the maximum height reached by the handball, we utilize the kinematic equation: v² = u² + 2as. Here, v is the final velocity (0 m/s, as the ball momentarily stops at maximum height), u is the initial velocity (19.6 m/s), a is the acceleration (-9.8 m/s², due to gravity acting in the opposite direction), and s is the height we seek.Substituting these values in, we have: 0 = (19.6)² + 2*(-9.8)*s. Solving this equation for s yields: s = 19.62 / (2*9.8) = 20m.The correct answer is thus Option B: 20m. Options A, C, and D arise from various errors in applying the kinematic equations, such as incorrect arithmetic or misunderstanding the effect of gravity on vertical motion.

questions.text: A projectile is launched at point O and follows the path OPQRS, as shown, Air resistance may be neglected. Which statement is true for the projectile when it is at the highest point Q of its path?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The horizontal component of the projectiles acceleration is zero
options.explanation: This is true. The horizontal component of acceleration is zero at heighest point during projectile motion, and throughout the motion.

options.text: The kinetic energy of the projectile is zero
options.explanation: Kinetic energy is not zero at the highest point during projectile motion. The velocity of the projectile at any point is Vx + Vy = V. Since the horizontal velocity will remain conserved throughout the journey as Ucosθ, V can not be zero. Hence, kinetic energy can not be zero.

options.text: The horizontal component of the projectile's velocity is zero
options.explanation: This statement is erroneous. The horizontal velocity will remain conserved througout the journey as Ucosθ.

options.text: The momentum of the projectile is zero
options.explanation: Momentum is not zero at any point during projectile motion. The velocity of the projectile at any point is vx + vy = v. Since the horizontal velocity will remain conserved throughout the journey as vcosθ, v can not be zero. Hence, momentum can not be zero.

questions.explanation: The horizontal component of the projectile's acceleration is zero at point Q.

questions.text: The velocity time graph of body X and Y is shown in fig. The ratio of the acceleration of Y to the acceleration of X is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The angle made by Y with the x-axis is 60 degrees, hence tan(60) = √3. The angle made by X with the x-axis is 30 degrees, hence tan(30) = 1/√3. Therefore, the ratio of acceleration of Y to X is √3 / (1/√3) = 3:1.
This would imply that both bodies have the same acceleration, which is incorrect as their slopes (angles) are different.
This ratio suggests that Y has less acceleration than X, but the graph shows Y's slope is steeper, indicating greater acceleration.
This ratio is incorrect as it does not reflect the actual difference in the slopes of the lines in the graph.

questions.explanation: To determine the ratio of the accelerations, we analyze the slopes of the velocity-time graph. The slope of a velocity-time graph represents acceleration. Body Y has a slope corresponding to an angle of 60 degrees with the x-axis, where tan(60) = √3. Body X has a slope corresponding to an angle of 30 degrees with the x-axis, where tan(30) = 1/√3. The ratio of the slopes (and thus the accelerations) is √3 / (1/√3) = 3:1, meaning Y accelerates three times faster than X.Other options suggest incorrect ratios based on misunderstandings of the graph's slopes.

questions.text: A cricket ball is hit so that it travels straight up in air and its acquires 3 seconds to each ball to reach the maximum height. Its initial velocity is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect. Using the equation v = u + at, where the final velocity v at maximum height is 0, does not give this result.
This is incorrect. Calculating using v = u + at with an acceleration of -9.8ms-2 for 3 seconds does not lead to this initial velocity.
v=u+at (v is 0 at max height) -u=at -u=-9.8 x3 u= 29.4m/s
This is incorrect. Solving the equation v = u + at with the given conditions does not yield this initial velocity.

questions.explanation: To determine the initial velocity of the cricket ball, we use the equation of motion: v = u + at. At the maximum height, the final velocity v is 0 m/s. Given that the ball takes 3 seconds to reach this height and the acceleration due to gravity a is -9.8ms-2, we can rearrange the equation to find the initial velocity u:0 = u + (-9.8ms-2)(3s)u = 9.8ms-2 * 3s = 29.4ms-1Thus, the initial velocity is 29.4ms-1. Other options are incorrect as they do not satisfy the equation with the given values.

questions.text: If 10 kg mass is dropped from a certain height, hits the ground with speed 10 ms-1. The height will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Calculating with conservation of energy principles gives a different result.
This is incorrect. You have overestimated the height by a factor of 2 due to a miscalculation in the energy conversion.
This option is incorrect. This height estimate does not match the energy conversion calculated using the given speed.
This is the correct answer. Using the potential and kinetic energy conversion gives this height.

questions.explanation: The problem involves the conversion of potential energy (P.E) to kinetic energy (K.E) as the mass falls. Initially, all energy is potential, given by P.E = mgh, where m is mass, g is gravity (approximately 9.8 m/s2), and h is the height. As it hits the ground, the energy is all kinetic, given by K.E = (1/2)mv2, where v is the velocity. Given m = 10 kg and v = 10 ms-1, we set the P.E equal to the K.E:mgh = (1/2)mv210 * 9.8 * h = (1/2) * 10 * (10)298h = 500h = 500 / 98 ≈ 5.1 mThus, the correct height is approximately 5 meters. The other options do not satisfy this energy balance and are incorrect.

questions.text: The velocity of a freely falling body changes as (g^p)*(h^q) where g is the acceleration due to gravity and h is the height. The values of p and q are:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Correct. The velocity of a freely falling body is proportional to the square root of the height (h^1/2) and the acceleration due to gravity (g^1/2).
Incorrect. This suggests that velocity is proportional to the height itself, not the square root of the height.
Incorrect. This implies velocity is directly proportional to gravity, not the square root of gravity.
Incorrect. This suggests that velocity is directly proportional to both gravity and height, which is incorrect for free fall.

questions.explanation: The velocity of a freely falling object can be derived from the kinematic equation: v = sqrt(2gh), where v is velocity, g is acceleration due to gravity, and h is height. This can be expressed as v = (g^1/2)*(h^1/2), indicating that p and q are both 1/2. Therefore, Option A is correct. Other options do not correctly represent the relationship between velocity, gravity, and height in free fall.

questions.text: If the time of flight of a projectile is 10 seconds. The range is 500m. The maximum height attained by it will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct solution. The maximum height can be calculated using the formula h = u²sin²θ/2g, which results in 125m.
This option is incorrect because it does not match the calculated maximum height based on the given values.
This option is incorrect as it does not align with the calculated maximum height using the provided information.
This option is incorrect as it does not correspond to the maximum height determined through the given data and calculations.

questions.explanation: Given that the range is 500m and the time of flight is 10 seconds, we can calculate the maximum height attained by the projectile using the formula h = u²sin²θ/2g, which results in 125m. This is the correct answer as it aligns with the provided data and calculations. The other options are incorrect as they do not match the calculated maximum height based on the given values.

questions.text: One fielder throws a ball and in 4 seconds it reaches to another player. So, the maximum height reached by the ball above the point of projection would be about:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect. This option does not align with the calculated maximum height.
Incorrect. This option does not align with the calculated maximum height.
Incorrect. This option does not align with the calculated maximum height.
This is the correct answer. By using the appropriate formula for projectile motion, the maximum height above the point of projection can be determined to be 20 meters.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 20 meters. This can be calculated using the appropriate equations for projectile motion and considering the time taken for the ball to reach the other player. The other options are incorrect as they do not align with the calculated maximum height.

questions.text: Two bodies are projected with the same velocity. If one is projected at an angle of 30o and the other at an angle of 60o to the horizontal, the ratio of the maximum heights reached is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as per the formula

This is the following solution:That's correct! Let's break it down:When two bodies are projected with the same initial velocity, the maximum height reached depends on the angle of projection.For the body projected at 30°:- The vertical component of the velocity is v₀sin(30°) = 0.5v₀- The maximum height reached is given by H₁ = (0.5v₀)² / (2g)For the body projected at 60°:- The vertical component of the velocity is v₀sin(60°) = 0.866v₀- The maximum height reached is given by H₂ = (0.866v₀)² / (2g)Now, let's find the ratio of the maximum heights:H₁ / H₂ = (0.5v₀)² / (0.866v₀)² = 0.25 / 0.75 = 1/3So, the ratio of the maximum heights reached is indeed 1:3.
Incorrect as per the formula
Incorrect as per the formula

questions.explanation: This is the correct solution:When comparing the maximum heights of two bodies projected at 30° and 60° to the horizontal with the same initial velocity, the ratio is 1:3.Explanation of other options:Option A, C, and D are incorrect as they do not consider the impact of the angle of projection on the vertical component of velocity and the maximum height reached. The correct ratio is 1:3 as explained in Option B.

questions.text: A projectile is projected with a kinetic energy K. Its range is R. It will have minimum kinetic energy, after covering a horizontal distance equal to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as the minimum kinetic energy is not at this distance.
Correct! The projectile possesses minimum kinetic energy at the highest point of the trajectory, which is at a horizontal distance of R/2.
Incorrect as the minimum kinetic energy is not at this distance.
Incorrect as the minimum kinetic energy is not at the end of the range.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B: 0.5 R. The projectile possesses minimum kinetic energy at the highest point of the trajectory, which is at a horizontal distance of R/2. At this point, the kinetic energy is at its minimum value before increasing again as the projectile moves downward. Options A, C, and D are incorrect as they do not correspond to the specific horizontal distance where the projectile's kinetic energy is minimum.

questions.text: Two balls are projected from the top of a tower of height 120 m, one with an initial upward velocity of 36 km/h and the other with an initial downward velocity of 36 km/h. The speed of each ball just before hitting the ground is: (take g = 10 m/s2 )
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as the speed just before hitting the ground is not influenced by the initial direction of the velocity.
Correct! Both balls will have the same speed of 50 m/s just before hitting the ground.
Incorrect as the speed just before hitting the ground is independent of the initial direction of the velocity.
Incorrect as the speed just before hitting the ground is a result of the height of the tower and the gravitational acceleration, not the initial velocities.

questions.explanation: When solving projectile motion problems, the final speed just before hitting the ground is independent of the initial direction of the velocity. Both balls will have the same speed of 50 m/s before hitting the ground due to the height of the tower and the effect of gravity. The correct answer is Option B.

questions.text: A ball thrown by one player reaches the other in 2 sec. The maximum height attained by the ball above the point of projection will be about:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as the formula for maximum height is not applied correctly.
Incorrect as the formula for maximum height is not applied correctly.

This is the correct answer. The maximum height can be calculated using the formula: H = (V^2 * sin^2θ) / 2g, where V is the initial velocity, θ is the angle of projection, and g is the acceleration due to gravity. By substituting the given values and solving, the maximum height is found to be 5m.
Incorrect as the formula for maximum height is not applied correctly.

questions.explanation: The maximum height of the projectile can be determined by analyzing the vertical motion. In this case, since the time taken to reach the other player is 2 seconds, the time to reach the maximum height would be half of this, which is 1 second. Using the formula for maximum height and substituting the given values, the correct answer is 5m. The other options are incorrect because they do not apply the correct formula or consider the given information accurately.

questions.text: A projectile is fired at 30o with momentum p. Neglecting friction, the change in kinetic energy when it returns to the ground will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Correct! According to the law of conservation of energy, projectiles acquire the same kinetic energy when they come at the same level in height. Since there is no air resistance and only conservative gravitational force is acting, the energy remains conserved. Hence, the change in kinetic energy when the projectile returns to the ground will be zero i.e. ΔK=0.

This option is incorrect. The change in kinetic energy is not 30% when the projectile returns to the ground. The percentage change in kinetic energy depends on the initial and final kinetic energies, which are typically not related to the launch angle.

This option is incorrect. The change in kinetic energy is not 60% when the projectile returns to the ground. The change in kinetic energy depends on other factors, such as the launch speed and the launch angle, and not a fixed percentage.

This option is incorrect. The change in kinetic energy is not 100% when the projectile returns to the ground. When the projectile is at ground level, it has a kinetic energy of zero (assuming no vertical motion), but it has kinetic energy during its flight.

questions.explanation: When a projectile is fired at 30° with momentum p, neglecting friction, the change in kinetic energy when it returns to the ground will be zero. This is because according to the law of conservation of energy, the total energy remains constant in the absence of external forces like air resistance. Therefore, the initial total energy equals the final total energy, resulting in a change in kinetic energy of zero. The other options are incorrect as they misrepresent the relationship between launch angle and change in kinetic energy.

questions.text: Two guns on a battleship simultaneously fire two shells at the same speed at enemy ships. If the shells follow the parabolic trajectories as shown, which ship will get hit first?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as both shells are fired at the same speed and follow parabolic trajectories.
Correct! The ship corresponding to the trajectory that reaches the target first will get hit first.
Incorrect as the shells travel different distances due to different trajectories.
Incorrect as the information provided is sufficient to determine which ship will get hit first based on the given conditions.

questions.explanation: When two objects are launched with the same initial speed, the object that follows the trajectory with a shallower angle will reach the target first. This is because the shallower trajectory covers a greater horizontal distance in the same time. Therefore, the ship corresponding to trajectory B will get hit first. The other options are incorrect because they do not consider the impact of different trajectories on the time it takes for the shells to reach their targets.
hints: which ship will get hit first
